lessons.title: Overview of the Module System
lessons.summary: Learn about the background and basic concepts of the module system introduced in Java 9. Understand everything from the problems with the classpath to the role of module-info.java.

contents.title: The Background Behind the Module System
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Why the [[module]] system (JPMS) was introduced
- The basic concepts of **modules** (explicit dependency management)
- The difference between the [[class]]path and the module path

Let's understand the major transformation introduced in Java 9.

contents.title: The Role of module-info.java
contents.body:
To define a module, place a **module-info.java** file at the root of the source tree.
In this file, you declare the module name, packages to export, dependent modules, and more.
Module names typically use a reverse-domain dot-separated format, similar to package names.
module-info.java is [[compiled]] into module-info.class and stored at the root of the JAR.
A JAR without this file is treated as an "Unnamed Module."
contents.note: **module-info.java** is placed in the root directory of the module. It should NOT be placed inside a **package**.

contents.title: Basics of exports and requires
contents.body:
The exports directive specifies packages to expose outside the module.
Packages not specified with exports cannot be accessed from outside the module.
The requires directive declares other modules that this module depends on.
Packages from modules not declared with requires cannot be used.
This mechanism makes dependencies explicit and prevents access to unnecessary classes.

contents.title: Benefits of the Module System
contents.body:
The main benefits of the module system are as follows.
The first is "strong [[encapsulation]]." Packages not declared with exports are completely hidden from external access.
The second is "explicit dependencies." The requires declaration enables dependency verification at compile time.
The third is "improved reliability." The module graph is resolved at startup, enabling early detection of missing dependencies.
The fourth is "custom runtimes." The jlink tool can create runtimes containing only the necessary modules.
contents.note: With the module system, even public classes cannot be accessed externally unless they are exported.

contents.title: Exam Preparation Tips
contents.body:
The certification exam tests the basic syntax of **module-info.java**.
It is especially important to understand the difference between exports and requires precisely.
exports is a declaration that "exposes your own packages to external modules."
requires is a declaration that "depends on another module."
Be careful not to confuse these two.
Also remember that the java.base module is a special module that is implicitly required.
contents.note: 【Practical Tips】In real-world development, many projects do not explicitly use the module system because Maven or Gradle handles dependency management. However, it indirectly affects areas such as access restrictions to JDK internal APIs.

quizzes.question: Which Java version introduced the module system?
quizzes.explanation: The module system (Project Jigsaw) was introduced in Java 9. It enabled package-level encapsulation and explicit dependency management.

quizzes.question: Which directive is used to specify packages to expose externally in a module definition?
quizzes.explanation: The exports directive is used to expose a module's packages externally. requires is used for declaring dependent modules, opens for reflection access, and provides for declaring service providers.

quizzes.question: The file name used to define a module is ______.
quizzes.explanation: Module definitions are written in the module-info.java file. This file is placed in the root directory of the module's source tree and declares the module name, exported packages, and dependencies.

lessons.title: Defining and Using Modules
lessons.summary: Learn the detailed syntax of module-info.java and advanced directives such as requires transitive and exports...to.

contents.title: The Complete Picture of Module Definitions
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The syntax and placement of **module-info.java**
- requires (dependency declaration) and exports (export declaration)
- Module operations using command-line tools

Learn the practical way to write [[module]] definitions.

contents.title: requires and requires transitive
contents.body:
requires declares modules that are directly depended upon.
Using requires transitive propagates the dependency to modules that depend on this module.
For example, if module A declares requires transitive B, module C that depends on A can also use B.
This means module C can access B's exported packages without explicitly requiring B.
transitive is called a transitive dependency and is particularly useful in library development.

contents.title: Key Directives in module-info.java
contents.body:
| Directive | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| requires | Declares a dependent module | requires java.sql; |
| exports | Exports a package externally | exports com.example.api; |
| requires transitive | Transitive dependency | requires transitive java.logging; |
| opens | Allows reflection access | opens com.example.model; |
| provides...with | Declares a service provider | provides MyService with MyImpl; |

The java.base module is automatically required, so it does not need to be explicitly declared.

contents.title: Qualified Exports with exports...to
contents.body:
A regular exports exposes a package to all modules, but exports...to allows you to expose it only to specific modules.
This lets you expose internal APIs to specific modules while prohibiting access from all others.
You can specify multiple modules after to, separated by commas.
This mechanism is called a "Qualified Export."

contents.title: The opens Directive
contents.body:
The opens directive is used to allow runtime [[reflection]] access.
While exports allows access at both [[compile]] time and runtime, opens only allows runtime reflection access.
This is needed when frameworks (JPA, Spring, etc.) access [[classes]] via reflection.
Declaring an open module exposes the entire module to reflection.
However, it is generally recommended to use opens declarations to expose only the necessary packages.
contents.note: exports allows compile-time + runtime access, while opens allows only runtime reflection access.

contents.title: Inter-module Dependencies and Compilation
contents.body:
Dependencies between modules are verified at compile time.
Using types from a module not declared with requires results in a compile error.
Circular dependencies (module A depends on B, and B depends on A) are not allowed.
Also, having the same package in multiple modules — known as "split packages" — results in an error.
These constraints ensure that dependencies between modules are clearly managed.

quizzes.question: To propagate module A's dependency to modules that depend on A, use requires ______.
quizzes.explanation: Using requires transitive declares a transitive dependency. When module A declares requires transitive B, modules that depend on A can also access B's exported packages.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes the exports...to directive?
quizzes.explanation: exports...to is known as a Qualified Export, which exposes a package only to specified modules. In this example, the com.example.lib.internal package is accessible only to the com.example.app module and cannot be accessed by other modules.

choices.text: Exposes the internal package to all modules
Exposes the internal package only to the com.example.app module
Allows reflection access from the com.example.app module
Declares a dependency on the com.example.app module

quizzes.question: What happens when com.example.app is compiled with the following module configuration?
quizzes.explanation: The com.example.lib.internal package is not exported, so it cannot be accessed from other modules. When com.example.app tries to use classes from this package, a compile error occurs. Only the com.example.lib.api package exposed with exports is accessible.

choices.text: A warning is issued but compilation succeeds

quizzes.question: Complete the code to define a module in module-info.java.
quizzes.explanation: The module keyword declares a module, and requires specifies dependent modules. This is a fundamental part of the module system introduced in Java 9.

quizzes.question: Select all directives that can be used in module-info.java.
quizzes.explanation: The directives available in module-info.java are requires, exports, opens, provides, and uses. imports is not a module directive.

lessons.title: Key JDK Modules
lessons.summary: Learn about the structure of JDK standard modules starting with java.base, and how to create custom runtimes using jlink.

contents.title: The Overall Structure of JDK Modules
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- The java.base module (automatic dependency)
- Key modules such as java.sql and java.logging
- Dependencies among JDK modules

Let's understand the module structure of the JDK itself.

contents.title: The java.base Module
contents.body:
java.base is the most fundamental module of the Java platform.
All modules implicitly depend on java.base, so you do not need to write requires java.base.
The main packages included in java.base are as follows.
It includes java.lang ([[String]], Object, System, etc.), java.util (List, Map, etc.), and java.io (File, [[InputStream]], etc.).
It also includes java.math, java.net, java.nio, java.time, and more.
These packages are always available without a requires declaration.
contents.note: java.base is implicitly required by all modules. You may write it explicitly, but it is redundant.

contents.title: Major JDK Modules
contents.body:
The JDK provides modules organized by purpose.
java.sql provides [[classes]] related to JDBC (database access).
java.logging provides Java's standard logging API.
java.desktop contains GUI-related classes such as Swing/AWT.
java.net provides network-related APIs such as HTTP Client.
To use these modules, you must explicitly declare requires in **module-info.java**.

contents.title: Custom Runtimes with jlink
contents.body:
jlink is a tool introduced in Java 9 that creates custom runtime images containing only the required modules.
A standard JDK can be several hundred megabytes, but jlink can generate a minimal runtime.
This significantly reduces the size of application distributions.
It is especially useful in resource-constrained environments such as container environments and IoT devices.
For jlink to work properly, the application must be modularized.
contents.code:
# Example of creating a custom runtime
jlink \
  --module-path $JAVA_HOME/jmods:mods \
  --add-modules com.example.app \
  --output custom-runtime

# Run the app with the created runtime
custom-runtime/bin/java \
  -m com.example.app/com.example.app.Main

# Check the runtime size
du -sh custom-runtime/

contents.title: Exam Preparation for Modules
contents.body:
The certification exam tests basic knowledge of JDK modules.
First, make sure to remember that java.base is implicitly required.
The packages included in java.base (java.lang, java.util, java.io, etc.) are frequently tested.
It is also important to know that modules like java.sql and java.logging require explicit requires declarations.
Command-line options may also be tested.
Organize options such as --module-path, -m (--module), and --add-modules.

quizzes.question: Which of the following correctly describes the java.base module?
quizzes.explanation: The java.base module is a special module that is implicitly required by all modules. It contains fundamental packages such as java.lang, java.util, and java.io, and does not need to be explicitly declared with requires.

choices.text: An explicit requires declaration is needed to use it
It is implicitly required by all modules
It is a module added in Java 11

quizzes.question: Which is the correct module-info.java for an application that uses JDBC?
quizzes.explanation: JDBC classes (java.sql.Connection, java.sql.DriverManager, etc.) are contained in the java.sql module. Unlike java.base, you must explicitly declare requires java.sql. Modules named java.jdbc or java.database do not exist.

quizzes.question: The JDK tool used to create a custom runtime image containing only the required modules is ______.
quizzes.explanation: jlink is a tool introduced in Java 9 that creates a minimal runtime image containing only the specified modules and their dependencies. This helps reduce the size of application distributions.
